detail: Tagged A4.T1 (composite/subtractive figures) but stem describes two similar rectangles with linear scale factor and asks for a corresponding side. This is A3.T3 (similar polygons, linear-side ratio). No combine/subtract operation present.

A2.T2 is cylinder volume/dimension. Clone uses a triangular prism with V=Bh. Stem structure (find base area from V and h via formula given) is correct geometry but wrong type — belongs under prism volume (A2.T1 family), not cylinder.

Stem describes a right circular cone (V=(1/3)πr²h), but template A2.T2 is cylinder (V=πr²h). The clone fits A2.T3 (cone/sphere/hemisphere). Math is correct (r=6) under cone formula, but type tag is wrong.

Clone tests subject-verb punctuation (no comma between long subject and main verb 'were highlighted'), not restrictive/nonrestrictive modifier choice required by A2.T5. No modifier-vs-noun distinction at the blank.

Clone tests A1.T1 clause-join (period vs comma vs run-on between two independent clauses: 'Bo Bardi was central figure in Brazilian modernism. As illustrated...Bo Bardi's designs were unmistakably populist'), not A2.T5 nonrestrictive/restrictive modifier punctuation.
Clone tests A1.T3 hard (conjunctive adverb 'however' at clause boundary requiring semicolon+comma combo per hard lever), not A2.T4 appositive/parenthetical set-off.

Clone tests A4.T10 colon-introduces-elaboration (complete clause 'Research...Ocean' followed by noun-phrase elaboration 'enormous reservoirs...'), not A3.T7 unique-title+name appositive. No proper-noun name is being set off.

Clone tests verb-object boundary (no punctuation between 'will recognize' and its object), not T5 nonrestrictive/restrictive modifier punctuation at a noun-modifier boundary. Rationale itself frames it as verb-object, not modifier.

Stem requires comma before 'such as' supplementary phrase, not paired punctuation around an appositive. T4 requires matched paired marks; only one edge needed here. Closer fit is A2 generic comma usage, not T4 appositive-pair.
Tests subject-verb non-separation (no modifier present). T5 requires restrictive/nonrestrictive modifier punctuation; this stem has no modifier element.
Tests comma after introductory subordinate clause (A1.T3 territory), not setting off an appositive/parenthetical with paired marks per T4.
Tests verb-object non-separation; no modifier present. T5 requires restrictive vs nonrestrictive modifier decision.
Tests preposition-complement non-separation, not restrictive/nonrestrictive modifier punctuation.
Tests preposition-complement non-separation; no modifier element to evaluate restrictive vs nonrestrictive.
Tests paired-comma enclosure of supplementary phrase 'among other functions' (A2.T4 appositive/parenthetical), not restrictive vs nonrestrictive modifier choice.
Tests verb-object non-separation, not restrictive/nonrestrictive modifier punctuation.
Tests preposition-complement non-separation; no modifier to evaluate.
Tests paired-dash appositive (A2.T4); 'Ecuador' followed by 'a country in South America' is general appositive, not unique-title+name pattern of T7.

Stem tests comma after introductory prepositional phrase, not A3.T7's unique-descriptor + name nonrestrictive appositive pattern. Answer (Ocean, the) is correct but the pattern doesn't match T7's core_skill_demand.
Tagged A2.T4 (set off appositive/parenthetical) but stem tests A5.T11 direct-question word order ('How ______' with inverted vs declarative + ?/. options). Answer correct.

Stem tests intro subordinate clause + main clause boundary (A1.T3 pattern), not setting off an appositive/parenthetical within a clause. The '(1967)' is internal to the intro clause; the keyed comma marks the intro/main boundary.
No restrictive/nonrestrictive modifier is at issue; the blank tests subject-verb punctuation ('authors [blank] have drawn'). Answer (no punctuation) is correct but the type is not A2.T5.
Blank tests verb-complement punctuation ('is named [blank] the chloroplast'), not a restrictive vs nonrestrictive modifier choice. Answer is correct but type is mis-tagged.
Blank tests preposition-complement punctuation ('known for [blank] some of the clearest waters'), not a modifier restrictive/nonrestrictive contrast. Answer correct but type mis-tagged.

Stem 'which point does Nora most directly make about herself' is T7-style (what point/question the text makes about a referent), not T8 character-trait inference. Answer B is correct and defensible, but the type tag should be T7.

Stem 'most strongly suggest about the reggaeton genre' targets the whole-passage thesis (genre underestimated despite substantive engagement). Correct answer B paraphrases the passage's overall claim, fitting A1 main-idea synthesis rather than A3 single-detail inference. Answer is correct.

Answer C ('permits scanning without subjecting surfaces to as much wear from contact') is essentially a paraphrase of the explicit sentence stating the coating absorbs wear rather than the sample. The item functions as A2 factual lookup rather than a one-step inference; no qualifier-discrimination characteristic of T6 Hard is present.

The elongated asteroid's direction (same as majority) and shape (elongated) are explicitly stated in the final sentence; answer B is direct factual lookup, not a one-step inference. Functions as A2.T4, not A3.T6, and lacks Hard T6's hedged/qualifier-discrimination feature.
Stem asks sum of central angles around a point (360° − 70 − 85 − 110 = 95). This is not a degree-radian conversion or combined-angle conversion (T9 core demand). Answer is correct (95°).

Stem asks for the y-coordinate of the center—a direct read from standard form (T1). T3 requires substitute/inequality reasoning to test whether a point lies on/inside the circle. Answer (8) is correct.
Stem asks for sector AREA (100π/4 = 25π), not arc length or arc-fraction of circumference. T7 core demand is the arc/circumference = θ/360 proportion. Answer (25π) is correct.

Stem asks which finding would 'most directly weaken' the claim, which matches A4.T6 (Finding-weakens-or-challenges-claim), not A4.T5 (support). Keyed answer C correctly weakens, so item itself is sound but templateId tags the wrong type.

Stem and stimulus use the T8 pattern (stimulus ends in '______' and stem asks 'Which choice... completes the student's conclusion'). T9 requires the explicit stem form 'Which choice best describes data... that support [conclusion]?' with no blank. Answer B is data-correct, but the type tag should be T8.

Stimulus ends in '______' and stem says 'Which choice most effectively uses data... to complete the text?' — this is the T8 sentence-completion structure, not T9 ('Which choice best describes data... that support [conclusion]?'). Answer B is data-correct, but the type tag should be T8.

Tagged as T7 (direct-data-readout, expects 'According to the table, what is X?' question). Clone's stimulus ends with blank '______' and stem asks 'Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to complete the statement?'—this is T8 (data-completes-sentence-with-value) structure, not T7.

Stem 'author of Text 2 would most likely agree with which statement about the description in Text 1' is a one-sided A1-style predicted-response, not T6 joint inference. T6 requires 'if both authors were aware of both findings' framing and a synthesis combining specific data from each text. Clone reads as A1.T1 (respond to overall description).

T3 requires stem verb 'characterize/regard/describe/say about' and answer options opening with an evaluative adjective/label ('As questionable...', 'As illogical...'). Clone uses stem 'how would Mendoza most likely respond to the underlined claim' and options begin with action gerunds ('By insisting', 'By acknowledging', 'By cautioning', 'By proposing'). This is T2 structure (respond to underlined claim). Additionally, no underline markup is present in the stimulus, violating T2 must_preserve as well.

Stem uses 'how would the author of Text 2 most likely regard the perspective of...' and answer options open with evaluative labels ('As shortsighted', 'As mistaken', 'As inconsistent', 'As unrealistic') followed by 'because' clauses. This is the T3 (characterize claim with evaluative label) structure, not T1 (respond to entire argument).

Stem explicitly references 'the interpretation presented in the underlined portion of Text 1' and Text 1 contains an underlined sentence. This is T2 (respond to underlined/specific claim), not T1 (respond to entire argument with no underlined portion). T1 must_preserve requires 'no underlined portion'.

Stem verb is 'regard' and answer options open with evaluative labels ('As surprising', 'As unfair', 'As problematic', 'As predictable') followed by 'because' clauses. This is the T3 (characterize with evaluative label) structure, not T2. T3 stem-verb marker (characterize/regard/describe) is explicit in template.
Stem is a difference-of-squares factoring/cancellation, not an exponent/radical-rule application. Better fits A2.T5 or A3.T8. Answer x^5-5 is correct.
Deliverable is a numeric value of an unknown constant c via coefficient matching; per template skill_wide_notes this belongs to A4.T9, not A1.T10 substitute-and-simplify. Answer c=1 is correct.

Item asks to identify b^(7/15) rewritten by multiplying numerator and denominator of the exponent by 15; no actual exponent/radical rule is exercised (just multiplying the fraction by 1). Stem is trivial and doesn't match T7 medium characteristics. Answer (b) is mathematically equivalent but the cognitive demand is mismatched.

Deliverable is a numeric value of unknown constant c via matching to cy^4-11; per template skill_wide_notes this belongs to A4.T9 (16de54c7 example in T9), not A1.T2 simple add/subtract. Answer c=10 is correct.

Stem '9z^5 + 4 - 2z^5 - 3' is combining like terms (T1), not adding/subtracting two parenthesized polynomials. Violates T2 must_preserve: 'parenthesised polynomial structure with explicit + or - between them'. Should be T1.
Stem '5(x-2) + 3(2x-4)' tests A1 distribute-and-combine, not A3 exponent/radical rules. No exponent or radical operation appears. Should be A1.T3 or A1.T2.

Deliverable is a numeric constant b (an unknown in target form ax^2+bx+c). Per skill-wide notes, when answer is a named constant the item belongs to A4. T6 medium characteristic explicitly says 'answer is the full expanded form'. Should be A4.T9.

T3 requires 'single outside factor times a parenthesised polynomial' (must_preserve). Clone is (p-q/3)² which is squaring a binomial — polynomial-times-polynomial, fitting T6 (multiply binomials), not T3 (distribute single factor). Answer B is mathematically correct, but stem structure violates T3 must_preserve.

Clone tests rational expression simplification via polynomial factoring and cancellation, which is T8 (rational expression simplification). T7 must_preserve requires 'exponent or radical structure as the core operation'; clone has no exponent/radical rules — it's straight factor-and-cancel. Answer A is mathematically correct.

Stem is distribute-and-combine to match at^2+b (no binomial-by-binomial multiplication). T6 requires multi-factor polynomial product; this fits A4.T9 (find a+b) with A1.T3 rewrite. Answer 3980 is correct.
Stem asks to factor 7y+21 into 7(y+3); this is GCF factoring (A2.T4), not distribution (A1.T3).
Stem asks to factor 6n+14 into 2(3n+7); this is GCF factoring (A2.T4), not distribution (A1.T3).
Stem (x+3)(x+4) is binomial multiplication (A2.T6 medium), not single-factor distribution; T6 also does not appear at easy.

Tagged A3.T8 (apostrophe/possessive) but options ('paintings were' / 'paintings,' / 'paintings, were' / 'paintings had been') vary in punctuation and finite-vs-nonfinite verb form, not in apostrophe placement. This tests A1.T2 (finite vs non-finite form) — main clause already has 'had been...overlooked,' so the blank must leave 'celebrated' as a non-finite participle inside a supplementary element offset by commas.

Tagged A2.T3 (subject-verb agreement) but the four options ('was' / 'is' / 'has been' / 'will be') all share singular number and differ only in tense. This is A1.T1 (tense selection from time cues) — answered correctly via the 1312-1337 date anchor, not via number agreement.

Tagged A2.T3 (subject-verb agreement) but options ('broadcasting' / 'having broadcast' / 'to broadcast' / 'broadcast to') vary in finite vs non-finite verb form, not in number. This is A1.T2 (finite vs non-finite form) — the main clause lacks a finite verb, so only the finite 'broadcast to' fits.

Stem has no interrupting modifier between subject 'demonstration' and verb — the blank sits inside a 'that' relative clause whose subject is 'that' (=demonstration). This is T3-like (or arguably A1, since options mix tense AND number). Answer 'advances' is correct.
Options vary in finite/non-finite form (perfect infinitive, participle, finite past, to-infinitive), not in tense. This matches T2 (Finite vs non-finite form), not T1 (Tense selection).

T2 requires the sample-level statistic (mean) to be computed from a list or two-way table. Clone gives a direct count (230 of 500) which is a simple proportion scale-up, matching T1 rather than T2. Medium T2 lever 'requires reading a two-way table' is not met.

Stem is not a causal/mechanistic explanation of an observed phenomenon (T3). Rather, it asks what property a replacement technology must have given stated limitations of pigeon dispatches — a comparative-contrast/implication structure (better fit: A4.T10) or a principle-applied prediction (A1.T1). No 'observation + proposed mechanism → infer underlying cause' structure required by T3. Answer D is correct logically; only the type tag is wrong.

No 'however/unlike/by contrast/whereas' pivot between two parallel sides demanded by T10. The inference is chronological reasoning from dated historical sources (manuscript ~1450 vs Munday 1598) — material/textual evidence with dates → past-tense historical claim, which matches A4.T9 (Historical/archaeological evidence scope) hard variant exactly (≥2 dates, transmission/origin reasoning). Answer C is correct; tag should be A4.T9.hard.

Stem reads as principle-application (A1.T1): eye contact signals attentiveness/respect, therefore engineers want natural eye contact to put customers at ease. No measured empirical comparison/observation as A4.T8 requires; correct answer is a forward deductive prediction, not a study-finding generalization.

Stem '3r+140=200' is a two-step equation (subtract 140, divide by 3) — belongs to T2, not T1. T1 must_preserve: 'Exactly one inverse operation between the equation as written and x=answer'; this clone requires two.

Clone asks for the difference of two coefficients (32.90 - 32.40 = 0.50), which is interpreting per-unit rates from the equation (A3.T3/T4), not substituting a given value and solving (A2.T2). No value is supplied for either variable.

Question asks for interpretation of a point (5,9) on a graph, not interpretation of a coefficient in an equation. A3 description requires explaining what 'a specific number or term in the equation' represents (rate, total, count). Interpreting coordinates of a point fits A5-style coordinate reading rather than A3. T3 specifically requires recognizing a coefficient as per-unit rate; no coefficient is being interpreted here. Keyed answer (length 5, width 9) is correct.
Stem contains no parallel/perpendicular partner line; it's a single line through two points with substitution. Belongs to A5.T12 (coordinate on a line), not A6.
Stem asks for difference of per-token point values by reading coefficients (30-8=22). No substitution into the equation; this is A3.T3 (interpret coefficients as per-unit rates), not A2.T2.
Stem requires reading coefficients (20-12=8). No substitution of a given variable value into the equation; fits A3.T3 (interpret coefficients), not A2.T2.

Stem is plain 'find x-intercept' from a table that already contains x=0 (y-intercept handed over). No translation/transformation or multi-step combination required; this is T11 easy/medium, not T12 hard, which requires derive-line + translate + report-coordinate.

Stem invokes 'infinitely many solutions' (identical-line/equivalent equations), not a parallel or perpendicular relationship. A6/T15 requires a parallel or perpendicular pair, not equation-equivalence.

Stem asks for the slope of 9x+4y=-53, not an x- or y-intercept. T11 is intercept extraction; slope extraction is T10. Also lacks the hard T11 levers (translation, ratio of intercepts, rearranged messy form).

Stem asks to interpret a solution pair (x,y), not a coefficient/product/constant term. The keyed answer requires substituting x=24 and solving for y, which is A2/T2 behavior, not A3/T4 (which interprets a piece of the equation in everyday language).

Original T7 requires writing equation from a graph. Clone gives three explicit points and asks which table lists them; the correct option is literally the three given points, so no slope/intercept computation is needed. Stem structure no longer matches T7's core_skill_demand.

T1 must_preserve requires 'two distinct per-unit rates attached to two distinct variables, summed to a stated total'. Clone is R = P + 38 (one variable plus a constant additive); no per-unit rate multipliers and only one variable on the right. Fits A2-style relation, not T1 two-rate equation.

T3 description: 'Interpret a coefficient as a per-unit rate' (the value of one unit of x). Clone asks to interpret the constant 90 (the total c in m + r = 90), not a per-unit rate coefficient. This task structure fits T4 (interpret a constant total) — not T3.

Stem gives initial value (14.70) + rate (0.43 per foot) but asks student to compute total pressure at x=80, a numeric output. That is A1 (evaluate/compute), not A3 (construct the equation). The student never writes the equation; answer choices are numbers, not equations.

No initial value present; pure proportional rate (saves $60/week, asks total in 7 weeks = $420). Answer is a number, not an equation. Violates A3.T3 must_preserve (initial value AND rate) and outputs a number, so this is A1 territory, not A3.

T1 requires substituting a single given numeric input into f and producing a numeric output. Here no input is given; the student selects a table that matches the constant function f(x)=52. Violates must_preserve 'A single numeric input value is given' and 'Answer is a single numeric output'. This is a table-identification stem, not an evaluate-f(c) stem.

A3.T3 must_preserve states 'Answer is the full equation in the variables named by the stem'. Clone asks for a numeric value (3.3), not an equation — this is A1.T1 (evaluate from given linear model) after a trivial verbal-to-equation step.
Stem asks meaning of the slope coefficient 4 in L(w)=4w+9 (cm stretched per newton). That is A2.T2 (interpret slope/rate), not A2.T1 (interpret constant term/initial value, which would be 9 here).
Stem asks for g(4) given g(x)=5x — this is evaluating f(c) at a given input (A1.T1), not solving f(x)=k for x (A1.T2). Answer 20 is correct, but type tag is wrong.
Tagged A2.T1 (interpret constant term) but stem asks to compute f(8)=19 — a numeric evaluation. This is A1.T1 (evaluate f(c)), not interpretation. Answer/rationale are correct.

Tagged A1.T1 (evaluate explicit f(x) at given input) but no f(x)=ax+b is supplied. Student must construct the model from words ($750 initial + $40/week × 5 weeks). This fits A3.T3 (build from rate + initial) or A1.T1 only after construction. Answer 950 is correct.
T2 = Solve f(x)=k for x. Clone evaluates p(64) directly (substitution), which is T1 (Evaluate f(c)), not T2. No target k to solve back to x.
T2 = Solve f(x)=k for x. Clone evaluates h(3) directly (substitution), which is T1 (Evaluate f(c)), not T2. No target value k provided to solve back for x.

A2.T2 must produce a numeric extremum, but this clone asks 'which inequality represents...' and the keyed answer is an inequality (15x+40y≤2950). This is an A1.T1 two-variable budget setup, not A2.T2.
Tagged A4 (match graph to inequality) but stem has no graph; it gives a textual inequality y<2x−3 and asks which ordered pair satisfies it. This is A3.T1 (point satisfies inequality).
Tagged A2.T2 (answer is a numeric max/min), but the clone's answer form is an inequality on s, not a number. This fits A1.T3 (multi-step contextual setup with percent overhead) or A5.T2, not A2.T2.

A5.T2 expects deriving an equivalent inequality or a numeric extremum from a given inequality/system. Clone asks for the interpretation of a single term (13q) in context, which is a coefficient-meaning item, not a solve/interpret-equivalent task.
Tagged A1.T3 (answer should be an inequality) but stem asks 'what is the greatest number m' with numeric SPR answer 24 — this fits A2 (solve for numeric extremum), not A1.

A3.T1 must_preserve: 'candidate pairs as the answer form' and 'requirement to satisfy every listed inequality'. Clone instead presents a single (L,W) pair and asks for interpretation; answer choices are sentences, not candidate ordered pairs. This is an interpretation task, not a 'point satisfies inequality' selection.

Tagged A1.T2 (range/bound on a single variable, answer = inequality). Clone asks for the minimum numeric rise (3.4°C) — a numerical extremum. This is an A2.T1 'have/need' task, not A1.T2. A1 must return an inequality; this returns a number.

Stem describes two lines m and t intersecting at a point with vertical angles — no parallel lines invoked. A1 requires explicit parallel-lines configuration (per must_preserve). This is A6 (intersecting lines at a point), not A1.

Stem is about similar triangles and proportional sides (EF length given scale factor 10/8). A6 is 'intersecting lines & angle relationships at a point' — no intersection configuration here. This belongs to A5.T2 (find side via proportional reasoning).

Stem asks for sin(40°) ratio in a right triangle — pure right-triangle trigonometry, no intersecting-lines-at-a-point configuration. A6.T1 covers vertical/linear-pair angle measure. Belongs to right-triangle trigonometry skill, not A6 of this skill.

A1.T2.medium requires both angles as linear expressions in parameter k. Clone gives one numeric angle (73°) and one variable x, with no parameter to solve for first. Stem matches A1.T1 (find unknown angle), not T2.
Clone tests triangle angle-sum (A2.T1): in triangle BGC use 180−42−70=68. No similarity reasoning, no AA, no shared-vertex similar triangles. Belongs to A2, not A5.T3.

Stem describes a triangle with adjacent angle relationships (vertical, supplementary, bisector), not two parallel lines with multi-transversal config. A1 requires explicit parallels; clone has none. Better fits A2 with A6 adjuncts.
No isosceles/equilateral side-equality invoked. Clone is intersecting lines (linear pair) + triangle angle sum. Belongs to A2/A6, not A3.
No similarity reasoning. Clone solves via linear-pair supplementary (138→42) then triangle angle sum (180−42−47=91). Belongs to A2.T3, not A5.T3.
Clone is exterior-angle theorem on a triangle (A2.T2), not vertical/linear-pair at an intersection of lines. A6 requires intersecting lines at a single point without triangle interior-sum invocation.
Clone is straight similarity-ratio side computation (A5.T2), not intersecting lines at a point. No vertical/linear-pair reasoning at all.
Solved by isosceles base angles (A3) + exterior-angle theorem (A2.T2). No AA similarity, no shared-vertex similar triangles. Should be A3.T1 or A2.T2, not A5.T3.

A6 requires intersecting lines at a single point with no triangle/parallel invocation. Clone is a right-triangle altitude / geometric-mean problem, which belongs to A5.T3 (right-triangle altitude similarity), not A6.
Clone is a triangle-area inequality (area = (1/2)·6·h), not an intersecting-lines / vertical-angle problem. Does not test A6 (intersecting lines at a point).

A3 requires isosceles/equilateral side-equality. Clone uses triangle similarity (angle transfer via similar triangles) with no isosceles side-equality — belongs to A5.T1 (angle transfer via similarity).

A6 boundary: 'no triangle sum'. Clone's core work is triangle interior sum (180-45-60=75), with vertical angle as a trivial final step. Better fits A2.T1 (find third angle) than A6.T1 (vertical/linear pair at a point).

A2.T3 requires a triangle interior-sum step plus a parallel transfer. Clone has no triangle: just BC∥AD with transversal AC and a single alternate-interior angle equality. Pure A1.T1 (parallels cut by transversal).

A1.T3 easy expects two parallel lines given with all four labeled angles and student picks the correct relationship statement. Clone reverses the logic: lines are NOT given parallel, and the task is to pick which equation proves they are parallel. This is a converse-of-parallel-postulate stem, not the templated relationship-statement stem.

Clone has only two intersecting lines (no parallel lines, no transversal). A1 requires explicit parallel-lines + transversal; this is A6 (vertical angles at a single intersection). T3 easy lever requires 'two parallel lines, one transversal, all four angles labeled.'

Clone asks how many solutions the system y=x²−4, y=0 has. This is A4 (system intersection count) or A2 (count for x²=4), not A3 — A3 requires a literal parameter controlling solution count. No parameter present.
Stem (3y+7)(4y−9)=0 is a factored zero-product form matching T6 (and the original 2926cc6d is listed under T6 example_ids), not T2 standard-form quadratic. Should be A2.T6.medium.

T10 must_preserve requires 'presence of a graph-described scenario (the visual cue is the load-bearing feature).' Clone removes the graph entirely and is solved purely algebraically; this drifts to A4.T9. Listed common_bug: 'Removing the graph and asking purely algebraically… changes the type to T9.'
Stem is zero-product (x+4)(x-9)=0, which is T6 (zero-product/factored polynomial), not T3 (absolute-value equation). Answer (-2) is mathematically correct, but typeId is wrong.

T10 requires a graph-described scenario (visual cue is load-bearing). Clone presents purely algebraic functions f(x) and g(x) with no graph; this is T9 (algebraic linear-quadratic substitution). Math is correct (a=3 or 12).
T10 requires graph-described scenario. Clone is purely algebraic |x+2|=x+4 system with no graph; this is T9 territory. Math correct: (-3,1).
T10 requires graph-described scenario. Clone presents equations algebraically and asks which point is an intersection; no graph shown. This is T9. Math correct: (4,4).
T10 requires graph-described scenario; clone is purely algebraic system substitution (no graph). Type should be T9. Math correct: (3,6).
Tagged A4.T10 (graph-based), but stem has no graph; it is a pure algebraic linear-quadratic substitution system, which is A4.T9.

Stem is a basic radical equation sqrt(x+3)=9 asking for the numeric root x=78. This tests A2 (solve a single nonlinear equation, radical type T4), not A5 (equivalent-form/reformulation). No reference to an equation 'above' nor any equivalent-form transformation. Matches documented common_bug: 'Drifting into A2 by asking for a single numeric root rather than an equivalent form or set.' Math is correct.

Clone asks for maximum height of a quadratic projectile h(t)=−5t²+30t+35 (max=80). This matches A3.T3 (vertex/extreme value of quadratic) or A4.T2 (quadratic projectile model), not A3.T5 (interval of increase, asymptote, symmetry). Answer 80 is correct.

Clone asks which evaluation g(c) equals the y-intercept k — pure definition of y-intercept = f(0). A5.T2 requires choosing among equivalent algebraic forms (standard/vertex/factored/rebased) that expose a feature. No equivalent forms are presented. Better fit: A3.T2 conceptual variant. Answer g(0) is correct.

Stem asks interpretation of h(0.8)=16.16, a function-value evaluation. That matches A2.T1 (Interpret f(c)=v statement), not A2.T2 (Interpret coefficient or constant). Answer C is correct; tag is wrong.
Stem asks f(-4) for f(x)=x^3-16x — direct substitution evaluation (A1.T1). Not graph-feature reasoning (A3.T5). f(-4)=-64+64=0, answer C correct, but aspect tag is wrong.

Stem gives g(x)=11·a^x with unknown a, condition g(2)=1331, asks g(3). This is parameter recovery + evaluation (A6.T1 medium lever), not building an exponential model from context (A4.T1). Answer 14641 = 11·11^3 is correct.

Geometric-decay bouncing-ball problem with iterative halving is closer to A4.T1 exponential decay context, but stem asks numeric height after 3 bounces (not building a model). Answer 4 ft correct (32→16→8→4).

Stem asks interpretation of x-intercept (a coordinate/feature point) — matches A2.T1 (Interpret a specific point/value in context), not A2.T2 (interpret a structural constant such as initial value or growth factor). Answer D correct.

A3.T3 is 'Vertex or extreme value of quadratic', but clone asks for an x-intercept of f(x)=x^2-20x-96. Stem and answer fit A3.T4 (x-intercepts/zeros of polynomial), not vertex/extremum. Answer (-4,0) is mathematically correct.
A3.T5 covers interval/asymptote/symmetry reasoning. Clone asks how many x-values satisfy f(x)=0 given two stated x-intercepts—this fits A3.T4 (x-intercepts/zeros). Answer 'Two' is correct.

Clone asks for interpretation of an x-intercept (p where P(q)=0) in context. This fits A3.T4 (x-intercepts/zeros) or A2.T2 (interpret constant), not A3.T3 (vertex/extreme value). Answer is correct (d).

Clone tests A6.T1 reasoning: solve for unknown parameters a, b, c from a horizontal asymptote and two passing points. A3.T5 targets graph-feature reasoning (intervals, asymptotes, equation-shape matching), not parameter recovery. Answer (Three) is mathematically correct.

Stem describes a numeric product f(n)=n(n-17), not a geometric area/volume context. T3 must_preserve requires 'geometric (or geometric-analog) context'; this clone is a context-free abstract product, fitting A4 only loosely. Answer is correct.

A6.T1 requires a nonlinear function form with unknown parameter(s) pinned by structural condition(s) (point/intercept/vertex). This clone is a pure system-of-equations identity (sum & sum-of-squares of two numbers) with no function f and no parameter to recover. Better fits algebra/system-of-equations skill, not nonlinear-functions A6.T1.

Stem asks for the numeric x-value that produces the maximum revenue (vertex of quadratic). That is A3.T3 (vertex/extreme value), not A2 interpretation. No 'best interpretation' phrasing or symbol-to-meaning mapping is required.

Stem builds an exponential decay model from a verbal percent-decrease description with no unknown parameter to solve. This is A4.T1 (build exponential growth/decay model), not A6.T1 (solve for parameter from structural condition).

Stem defines f(x)=2x^2-8 and asks to solve f(x)=24 (A1.T2 'Solve f(x)=k'); tagged as A3.T1 'Read intercept or point from graph' but no graph is involved and no intercept/coordinate is requested. Answer x=4 is correct, but aspect/type tag is wrong.

Clone is contextual exponential p(x)=8(1.05)^x asking for population at x=0 — A1.T1 (substitution into nonlinear rule) or A2.T2 (interpret initial-value constant), not A3.T1 (read intercept/point from a graph). No graph appears in the stem. Answer 8 is mathematically correct.

Stem gives the y-intercept (0,-4) and asks for f(0) — a trivial intercept-from-given-point task fitting A3.T1 (read intercept) or A3.T2 (intercept from equation), not A3.T5 'Other graph-feature reasoning' (asymptotes, intervals, symmetry). Answer -4 is correct.
Stem requires evaluating h(t)=-16t^2+64 at t=0,1,2 (direct substitution). This is A1.T1 (Direct substitution into f(x)), not A3 (graph feature reading).
Stem is direct substitution g(4)=3*16-5=43 — matches A1.T1 (Direct substitution into f(x)) rather than A3.T1 (read intercept/point from graph).
Stem asks for f(2) of f(x)=x^2+4 — this is direct substitution (A1.T1), not reading an intercept from a graph (A3.T1). No graph present and no intercept question.
Stem describes a vertical translation of a graph by 3 units and asks which point lies on the translated graph — this is A5.T1 (vertical/horizontal shift), not A3.T1 (read intercept/point from graph).
Stem asks for f(2) of f(x)=x^2+3 — direct substitution (A1.T1), not graph-feature identification (A3.T1). No graph, no intercept question.

Stem compares medians of two separate data sets (Year 2018 vs Year 2019). This is A4.T9 (compare measures across two data sets), not A5.T14 (effect of a manipulation on one set's statistics). No manipulation/transformation is described.

Question computes median from a frequency table (cumulative counting to middle position). A3.T7 is direct retrieval (min/max/sum of named rows), not computing a measure of center. This belongs in A1.T3 (median from frequency table).

A2.T5 requires qualitative SD comparison from two visual distributions. This stem compares two classes via min/max/median table and the keyed answer (B) is about which class contained the highest score — purely a min/max comparison. Belongs in A4.T9.

A3.T7 medium specifically targets identifying the min/max value with nonzero frequency (often with a zero-frequency distractor row). This clone asks for the mode of a frequency table — a different task with no zero-frequency distractor; loses the type's medium signature.

T5 requires qualitative SD comparison between two distributions; clone asks for the smallest possible positive difference between MEANS of two binned histograms, which is a mean-comparison item (A4) rather than an SD comparison. The keyed answer (10) is also not strictly determinable since values can be any integer within each bin, allowing mean differences other than 10.

Stem asks 'what percent greater' (percent change between two values), which is A3.T3, not A2.T1 ('what percent of N is M'). Answer 540% is mathematically correct for the percent-greater interpretation, but the tagged aspect/type doesn't match the cognitive operation.

Stem is a tip-on-bill scenario (surcharge add-on context), matching A1.T3 (tax/tip add-on) rather than A1.T2 generic 'p% of N' wrapper. Asks for surcharge amount alone, exactly matching T3 easy lever.

Stem asks 'what percent of customers gave 2 stars' (part=20, whole=200, find percent). This is A2.T1 (find percent from part/whole), not A1 (compute part from given percent). A1 requires the percent to be given.

Stem describes a 25% price reduction (before/after change), which is A3.T1 (find new value after percent decrease), not A1.T2 (single p%-of-N in real-world context). A3 boundary: 'two states of the same quantity are compared'.

Stem describes a 35% increase from 2010 to 2025 (before/after percent change), which is A3.T1 (find new value after stated percent change), not A1.T2 (percent of a whole). Common-bug warned against in A1.T2 must_preserve.

Clone asks 'Of those at least 30, what fraction were at least 40?' — this is a conditional probability (A3.T8 structure: numerator=15 in subset, denominator=27 subset total), not a 'closest to fraction × marginal' comparison (A5.T16). Answer 15/27 is mathematically correct, but the question tests conditional reading, not the T16 'compare candidates to target fraction' skill.

T7 is 'Marginal probability with SPR output' (favorable = one full row or column over grand total). Clone asks for the JOINT cell P(yellow AND square) = 40/200, which is T5 (joint cell), not T7 (marginal). Answer 1/5 is correct, but type tag is wrong.

Clone is a Reverse conditional — solve for missing cell (T10), not a forward conditional reading (T8). Stem states P(B) = 1/5 with unknown x in the denominator and asks to solve for x. Answer x=6 is correct, but the template tag should be A3.T10.hard.

Stem provides two quantities (32 oz pitcher, 128 oz gallon) but solving requires only a single division 128/32=4, not a chained conversion. Should be A1.T1, not A1.T3. Also the gallon-to-ounce equivalence is given explicitly in the stem rather than as the hard variant's implicit common-knowledge fact.

Tagged A2.T2 (Express missing ratio term symbolically) but stem uses pure numeric ratio (2:5, a=14) and asks for numeric b=35. Answer is numeric, violating T2 must_preserve 'Answer is an algebraic expression, not a numeric value'. This is A2.T1 (numeric proportion).
T4 requires an algebraic relationship in a rate/proportional context. Stem 'x/3 = 7' is pure linear equation solving with no rate or proportional scenario.
TemplateId says T2 (compound-rate conversion), but stem is a single-step linear unit conversion (48 yd → ft) with no rate/compound unit. This is A1.T1, not A1.T2.

T13 requires expression of form sin(a)cos(b)+cos(a)sin(b) with two complementary angles inside the expression. Clone is a cofunction reciprocal-tangent question within one right triangle, which fits T11 (cofunction value swap), not T13.

Clone has no graph; it's a pure algebraic solve (substitution to find (20,20)). This is A2.T5/A1.T2 territory, not A3 (graphical representation). Answer is correct, but A3 must_preserve 'one side graphical, one algebraic' is violated.

T8 requires the stem to ask how many solutions (categorical: 0/1/infinite). Clone asks for the intersection coordinates (6,2) — that is an A1/A3 task (solve system / read graph), not solution-counting. Violates T8 must_preserve: 'answer is a categorical choice about solution count'.

T7 requires one side of the question to be graphical (matching a graph to a system or vice versa). Clone presents two pairs of points (purely algebraic) and asks for the solution (x,y) directly—this is A1.T2 behavior (solve the system), not T7 (match system to graph).
Tagged T1 (one equation isolates a variable) but stem 'x+y=160; x+2y=215' has both equations with both variables — that is T2 structure.
Tagged T2 (both equations involve both variables) but u=4 already isolates a variable — that is T1 structure.
T6 must_preserve: 'graph of exactly two lines shown'. Clone has no graph; pure algebraic system — categorical drift to A1.
Stem asks for combined expression x-y rather than a raw component; that is T3 (solve and evaluate combined expression), not T1.

T7 must_preserve: 'one side of the question is graphical, the other algebraic'. Clone presents two algebraic equations and asks which candidate point is the solution — no graphical component; this is A1 territory.
T6 must_preserve: 'graph of exactly two lines shown'. No graph in stem; pure algebraic — categorical drift to A1.
T6 must_preserve: 'graph of exactly two lines shown'. Clone has no graph; pure algebraic system — categorical drift to A1.

T2 description requires that 'both equations involve both variables', but the clone's second equation y=17 is a single-variable equation that already isolates y. This is T1 (trivial one-equation isolates a variable) structure, not T2.
T2 requires both equations involve both variables; here y=22 already isolates y, making this a T1 (trivial one-equation isolates a variable) structure rather than T2.

Stem reads 'function of the underlined sentences' (plural) and the underlined span covers two sentences; T3 must_preserve requires 'Exactly one complete sentence underlined'. Multi-sentence span fits T4 better.

T8 requires ≥3 distinct rhetorical moves; clone has only 2 moves (introduce Hopper + describe compiler work; Abbate's judgment is a coda to the second move, not a third distinct move). Correct answer B explicitly names just 2-3 vague steps. Closer to T7 two-move structure.

Tagged T1 (direct contrast between two paired items) but stem is single-subject expectation reversal (Villa-Lobos's conventional surface vs. his innovation within it). Fits T2 (expectation reversal with 'nevertheless') better than T1.

T11 requires an intensifier (in fact/indeed) reinforcing the same claim. Keyed 'Again and again' signals repetition/frequency, not intensification. Does not match T11's core_skill_demand or must_preserve 'intensifier transition as unique correct option.'

T6 (reasoning chain) requires chain-step markers like 'in turn,' 'as such,' 'accordingly,' typically mid-sentence parentheticals. Keyed answer 'Consequently,' is a terminal-result marker (T4), not an intermediate chain link. Stem also lacks multi-step downstream chain; it's a single cause→conclusion.

T10 requires additive transitions (in addition/additionally/moreover/furthermore) on parallel independent points. Keyed 'Alternatively,' signals alternation (one option vs another), not addition. Violates T10 must_preserve 'additive transition as unique correct option.'

T3 (concession with caveat) requires three-part claim→concession→restoration structure with markers like 'Granted,' 'That said,' 'Of course,' 'Admittedly.' Stem is two-part expectation reversal (engineers believed X; even so, reality is Y) with no restoration. Fits T2 better than T3.

Template T6 (reasoning chain) requires chain-step markers like 'in turn', 'as such', 'accordingly' placed mid-sentence as parenthetical. Clone's keyed answer 'Consequently' is a terminal result marker characteristic of T4, placed sentence-initially. Matches common_bug: 'Cloner uses Therefore (terminal) when In turn (intermediate) is needed.' Stem is two-step, not multi-step chain.

Tagged A5.T15 (Earlier/Previously flashback) but clone has no date inversion and keyed answer is 'Ultimately,' — this is T16 culmination, not T15 backward-pointing temporal. Violates must_preserve: 'Two events with explicit dates whose order is reversed' and 'Backward-pointing temporal marker as unique correct option.'

Question asks for predicted y at x=30, which is T7 (predict y) logic, not T8 (residual = actual minus predicted). No actual data point given; only the line and an interpolated x. Answer math (≈6.3) is correct, but type is mistagged.

Question asks for predicted y at x=6 using the line of best fit — this is T7 logic (predict y at given x), not T8 (residual = actual minus predicted). No actual data point is given. Answer math (6.33→6) is correct, but type is mistagged.
Tagged A6.T12 (exponential curve parameter), but clone asks for slope of a linear line of best fit through (0,15) and (12,135). This is A3.T5 (compute slope), not A6 (exponential parameter).
Tagged A3.T6 (interpret slope in context) but clone asks for a predicted y-value at x=6 using the line, which is A4.T7 (predict y at given x). Not slope interpretation.
Tagged A3.T5 (compute slope of best-fit line) but clone asks for predicted y at x=900, which is A4.T7 (predict y at given x). Answer is correct (9), but aspect tag is wrong.
Clone reads a value directly from a data table with no line of best fit or prediction model. This is A1 (read raw values), not A4 (predict using model).
T10 requires classifying among {linear/exponential × increasing/decreasing} model families. Clone instead asks to describe the association direction; MCQ options do not span the 2x2 model grid.

T11 must_preserve requires a clearly nonlinear (typically exponential) scatterplot with curve options. Clone shows linear data with linear-vs-constant-vs-quadratic options; correct answer is 'increasing linear', so it fits A5.T10 not T11.
Clone reads weight directly from a listed data point (5, 50). No line of best fit or prediction model is used. This is A1 reading raw values, not A4 model-based prediction.
Clone looks up 50 mph row directly in the table (180 ft). No line of best fit or model prediction is applied; this fits A1, not A4.
T2 must_preserve requires a contextualized x-y graph (not a generic scatterplot or table). Clone uses a table of values, not a graph; structure better matches A1.T1 medium (interval-change reading).
T2 must_preserve requires a contextualized x-y graph. Clone presents only a verbal/bulleted description with no plotted graph, violating the structural requirement.
Tagged A1.T2 (qualitative graph reading, no equation/numeric prediction). Stem asks for predicted density via slope computation at x=55%—this is A4.T7 prediction, not A1 reading.

Tagged T2 (contextual graph, qualitative property). Stem uses a table (not graph) and asks for a numeric difference (200-90=110). Closer to T1 (compare values) but T2 must_preserve specifies 'contextualized x-y graph, not table' and 'no equation/numeric computation.'

Tagged as A1.T2 (multi-word phrase meaning) but only a single word 'contesting' is underlined — this is structurally A1.T1 (single-word meaning). Also violates A1.T2 must_preserve stem wording: clone uses A2-style 'Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?' instead of 'As used in the text, what does the phrase "X" most nearly mean?'. Answer 'Obstructing' is reasonable for 'contesting our passage'.

Tagged T8 (negation/scarcity/counterfactual frame) but stem has no negation around the blank: 'such ______ stimuli'. The blank is a plain adjective describing vibration/stroking, with 'less lasting relief' applying elsewhere. Structurally this is T6 (degree/quality descriptor), not T8.

Clone fits T8 (scarcity frame: 'there is a ______ studies') rather than T3 (restatement gloss). The blank is filled by 'scarcity of' — a paucity/scarcity frame is the canonical T8-hard pattern per template.
Cue is restatement ('cheap alternative') not a contrast signal; stem fits T3 better than T4.
No contrast/concession signal; cue is causal ('helped drive'). Fits T5 better than T4.
No contrast signal in stimulus; cue is restatement ('worked closely together'). Fits T3, not T4.

All four options are two-word verb+preposition phrases ('guided by', 'puzzled by', 'ignored by', 'displeased with'). This matches T7 (two-word/prepositional collocation), not T6 (single adjective descriptor).
T6 requires an adjective slot describing a noun's quality. Clone's blank is a verb ('provided') with object 'surprising clarity', matching T5 (cause/effect/function verb) rather than T6.
